# Objects & Data Structures

::: {.callout-important}

## Everything is an Object

In Swift, there are no "primitives" like in standard JavaScript. Every value is a complex type with its own properties and methods.
:::

- **Standard Integers are Objects:** An `Int` in Swift is actually a `struct` object type.
- Because it is an object, this is valid Swift code:

```swift
let greeting = 1.hello // Valid, assuming 'hello' is an extension on Int

```

- You can call methods directly on the number

```swift
1.hello() // Also valid

```


#### If numbers are objects, what happens if you build a custom object and try to add them together with `+`?


```swift
let objectOne = {description: "hey there", title: "number one"}
let objectTwo = {description: "two", title: "number two"}


objectOne + objectTwo // Compiler Error
```

::: {.callout-warning}

## Why does it fail? (Type Safety)

Swift’s strict Type Safety system refuses to guess what you want the `+` operator to do.

* *Do you want to mathematically add the numbers?*
* *Do you want to concatenate the title strings?*
* *Do you want to merge the descriptions?*

Because the compiler cannot logically know your intent, it safely fails.
:::

#### The Solution: Operator Overloading

- Explicitly teach the compiler how to add your custom types.
- This is done by writing a function for the `+` sign.

```swift
struct CustomNumber {
    var value: Int
    var title: String
    var description: String
}

extension CustomNumber {
    static func + (left: CustomNumber, right: CustomNumber) -> CustomNumber {
        
        // Define the exact logic for merging the properties
        let newValue = left.value + right.value
        let newTitle = left.title + " and " + right.title
        let newDescription = left.description + " | " + right.description
        
        return CustomNumber(value: newValue, title: newTitle, description: newDescription)
    }
}

let numberOne = CustomNumber(value: 1, title: "number one", description: "hey there")
let numberTwo = CustomNumber(value: 2, title: "number two", description: "two")

let result = numberOne + numberTwo

print(result.value)       // 3
print(result.title)       // "number one and number two"
print(result.description) // "hey there | two"

```

::: {.callout-caution}

## Is Operator Overloading Common?

* **Mathematical Types = Very Common:** Custom `Vector`, `Matrix`, or `Currency` structs almost always overload `+` to allow clean math syntax.
* **Complex UI/Data Types = Bad Practice:** Do not use `+` to merge User Profiles or UI Elements. Instead, write clear functions like `numberOne.combineWith(numberTwo)` so other developers don't have to guess the behavior.
:::

---


# The 4 Main Object Types in Swift

To model data accurately, Swift requires you to choose a structure that matches your **memory** and **concurrency** needs.


::: {.callout}
* `struct`: **Value** type. Copied when passed. (Default choice).
* `class`: **Reference** type. Shared when passed.
* `enum`: **Value** type. Represents distinct states, can hold associated data.
* `actor`: **Reference** type. Shared, but strictly **thread-safe**.


:::


#### Value Types vs Reference Types

![Scenario A: sending a copy — two completely independent memory allocations (Value Types)](../public/images/value-types.png)



![Scenario B: sending a link — both variables point to the same memory location (Reference Types)](../public/images/reference-types.png)

---

## Structs (Value Types)

The `struct` is the backbone of Swift and the default choice for data modeling (and all SwiftUI views).

::: {.callout-tip title="JS / React Perspective"}
React requires state to be immutable (never mutating variables directly; always using `setState`). Swift enforces immutability automatically with structs. When you pass a struct, Swift creates an entirely new, disconnected physical copy in memory.
:::

**Key Traits:**

* **Behavior:** Copied on assignment.
* **Safety:** Modifying a copy does not alter the original.
* **Inheritance:** None. (Use Protocols instead).

```swift
struct User {
    var name: String
    var role: String
}

var admin = User(name: "Alice", role: "Admin")
var backupAdmin = admin // Creates a distinct, physical copy in memory

backupAdmin.name = "Bob"
// admin.name safely remains "Alice"

```

---

##  Classes (Reference Types)

Classes are used less frequently in modern Swift than in older object-oriented languages. You use them primarily for data that *must* be shared across different screens simultaneously (like ViewModels).

::: {.callout-tip title="JS / React Perspective"}
A Swift `class` behaves exactly like a standard JavaScript Object. If you pass it to a new variable, you are just passing a pointer. Changing one changes them all.
:::

**Key Traits:**

* **Behavior:** Shared reference in memory.
* **Safety:** Mutable across your entire app (requires careful management).
* **Inheritance:** Can inherit from parent classes.

```swift
class DatabaseConnection {
    var isConnected: Bool = false
}

let db1 = DatabaseConnection()
let db2 = db1 // Points to the exact same memory address

db2.isConnected = true
// db1.isConnected is now also true

```

---

## Enums (Enumerations)

Enums in Swift are first-class, memory-safe structures used to represent a group of related, mutually exclusive states.

::: {.callout-tip title="JS / React Perspective"}
In TypeScript, you enforce state using Union Strings (`type Status = "success" | "error" | "loading"`). Swift enums are the native, highly-optimized equivalent, but they can also carry variables directly inside them.
:::

**Key Traits:**

* **Behavior:** Value type.
* **Superpower:** Can attach specific variables directly to specific cases ("Associated Values").
* **Usage:** Replaces boolean flags (`isLoading`, `hasError`) and magic strings.

```swift
enum LoadingState {
    case idle
    case loading
    case success([String]) // Data is attached safely inside the case
    case error(Error)
}

let currentState = LoadingState.success(["Item 1", "Item 2"])

```

---

##  Actors (Concurrency-Safe Reference Types)

Actors are a modern Swift feature built exclusively to prevent **Data Races** (when two parts of your app try to change the same memory at the exact same millisecond).

::: {.callout-tip title="JS / React Perspective"}
JavaScript is single-threaded; the Event Loop handles tasks one by one, so parallel memory collisions rarely happen. Swift is genuinely multi-threaded. Code runs in parallel. Actors are required to safely manage shared data across these parallel threads.
:::

**Key Traits:**

* **Behavior:** Reference type (Shared memory).
* **Safety:** Automatically isolates its state. It forces outside code to wait in a queue (`await`) to interact with it.
* **Usage:** High-performance, concurrent background tasks.

```swift
actor SafeCounter {
    var count = 0
    
    func increment() {
        count += 1
    }
}

let counter = SafeCounter()

// Because the actor is heavily guarded, you must pause and "await" your turn to access it:
// await counter.increment()

```
